title: Craig Mod: Vibe Coding Towards the Apocalypse | The Aboard Podcast 2026-05-26

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford

full_transcript.text: Yeah?

full_transcript.text: I love both of those things.

full_transcript.text: Me too.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let's do it.

full_transcript.text: Abord.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Richard.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What is our company? What do we do all day?

full_transcript.text: We are Abord. We are based in New York City, but we've got a team around the world that comes in, understands your organization or business, puts really talented people to sort of triangulate on what you need, and then we use all these incredible tools to solve problems for you. We're an AI transformation shop. We don't just come in and say, 'Come on, let's unleash the agents!' No, we don't do that. We think about what you need and then we're in all sorts of interesting places nowadays, Paul.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So first of all, things are going good. If you want to talk, it's a wonderful time to reach out. It's busy, the shop is busy. But you and I have shipped software for over two trillion years.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I'm going to tell you something about AI, you ready?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Go.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's software.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes, it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It is just software. It's weird, it's different, it's got a different shape, but if you can understand how to stand up a piece of software, you can actually get some of this exciting super-revolution into your org. So give us a yell. Abord is very reachable on the internet, on the web, via hello@abord.com. We'll give you that email again when we get out of this podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let's do it. What do we got here, Paul?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, we have an old friend of the, of sort of everything, many companies ago I think we started talking to this person. This is Craig Mod. Craig!

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Hello.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Welcome, Craig.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's good to have you here.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Thanks for having me.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I want you to explain who you are in a second, but I'm going to tell you the specific reason that we reached out, even though kind of we would have you in a minute, but it's nice to see you, was that you wrote a blog post about you - you reside in Japan and you have very complicated accounting issues as an American residing in Japan working for lots of different companies, mostly around the design and consultancy field. I remember when AI started, somebody took me aside and they were like, 'Could we build our own QuickBooks?' and I was like, 'Never do that, that's the worst idea I've ever heard.'

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then you went ahead and did it, and it worked really well for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so I think we just want to talk about what it's like to build your own accounting software, the most exciting subject that has ever been on earth.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I mean, way to set up a cliffhanger, Paul. Before you do that, Craig, tell us about yourself, but also tell us about your - the skills you feel like you're pretty comfortable with.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I mean, I've been programming...

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So I have this long technical background. And then I've been involved with books and publishing forever as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And so those are like the two things. Books, making books, making physical books and then doing technical stuff. I actually got a degree in computer science and fine arts. It was like this weird double thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Anyway, it was good. So I'm very comfortable in the land of technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: But you know, I had a fellowship at Fabrica. This is like this sort of digital art collective thing in Northern Italy.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm absolutely nodding as if I have any idea what that is.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Jonathan Harris, remember number 27? Anyway, like all the, Casey Reas, you know, John Maeda obviously, all the old digital art people. I was kind of like in that in that zone and trying to like emulate them and following them around the world. So I was following them, following McSweeney's and then Kindle hits, iPad hits, you know, and then you start thinking about digital books.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And then Amazon destroys that for you so you don't have to think about it anymore. It's great.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Done.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Just make physical books. Make nice physical books.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Back to physical.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: To help the listeners slash viewers a little bit, we think of you really as someone who is anchored in design as well as doing all these other things. So just push back on that. Is that true?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, it is. But I mean, I think my getting involved with it was sort of a hack to make money coming from a place that had really no money. And I saw, 'Oh well, if I can get good at this...'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Meaning your childhood.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Meaning my childhood. Yeah, yeah. And it was sort of like, 'Oh, if I can get good at the design side of...' and it was a little more accessible. Like there was no degrees for web design, you know, whereas there was computer science.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There are now.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: There are now. Yeah, but no, you're right.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And so I ended up getting involved in the design world mainly because I was like, 'Oh, this is a good hack to...' and I was, you know, drawn to it and was pretty good at it and good enough and you know, it just kind of made sense. But it was always kind of a way to get to these other things.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So to produce the art or to work on the books, because I was too afraid to write them. So I'll design the books, or I'll publish the books, and then finally working up to doing the writing and all that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That is... I had the same thing where it's archetypal broke kid thinking: 'They'll never let me in. I'll never be allowed to participate. But I'll just go stand kind of close to it just to see what's up.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then one day they're like, 'Do you want to write a book?' And you're like, 'Uh, okay.' Right?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: All right, so that's for everyone, that's the advice you need for a good career. Just be a little broken.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Just be incredibly down on yourself for an incredibly... No, a little hunger is a good thing. I think it's a... and a little curiosity is a good thing. And I came out of law school and I was like, 'Wait a minute, what's this internet thing?' It was exactly the same.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Wait, you went to law school?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I did.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This podcast is sponsored by Harvard Law School. No, went to law school and then came out and the internet just landed. I was like, 'I'm gonna go see what's going on over here.'

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And never came back. All right, let's fast forward.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Obviously the world changed a few years ago.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're clearly frustrated with your accounting software, which you could write a book about that.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Don't write a book about that.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's like you were just handing in one W-2.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Uh, let's frame it though. It's not...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Give us a sense. You wrote it. For people who prefer text, you can go to craigmod.com and read the whole story of his accounting software. But we're going to try to just jazz hands it a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Well a little bit of context too, like ChatGPT comes out and I'm like, okay this is interesting but you can't really build stuff with it and then you're like, oh but it can write little scripts for you? That's kind of cool. And then Claude Code comes out last year and I'm running a membership program connected with like my book stuff and writing stuff, and I've always hated Twitter.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Like since the day it launched I was sort of anti-Twitter even before Musk and blah blah blah. And I just, I always had this philosophy, this is how Twitter should work, and Claude Code came out and I was like, let me see if I can use Claude Code as like a test project to make my own Twitter for my community. And that was the first thing I built.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What people don't understand is that when you get annoyed and frustrated with things, you often stand up platforms. I've seen you do this like now more than once. And so yeah, this is a normal thing for you to just be like, I'm going to create a community platform and just see what happens.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well usually because I've been pushed into the corner because yeah, I feel like I don't have any other options, I gotta do it myself, you know.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're very gentle in tone, but you must get extremely annoyed. Because the average human isn't like, I should get a database going and set up my own publishing platform. I'll show them. So you used Claude a year ago, right? March last year it comes out, this is the first project, boom, clone Twitter.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you publish books, you have tours that you give, of walking tours around the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: No, I don't give tours. Absolutely not.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Insulted the man!

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I did not say that you take money for the tours. But you definitely do—

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I walk with people. It's called walking with friends, Paul. Just buds.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You enable walking communities through interesting geographies, but it doesn't— it's not a tour. No one should ever look in— so they can only look straight down the road.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Insulted our guest.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh my god. Okay, so non-tours, but you bring together communities to go around and do things and you are a very serious photographer as you go on these very long walks.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. I mean, most of the walks are solo. Like 30, 40 days alone, which is not indicative of anything broken or weird or— it's fine, let's not worry about it. It's called a guy going for a walk, Paul, just to clarify for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But what the whole point I was getting to before this debacle is that you are a publisher and you have a community around these sort of community-building non-tours. And that you have to manage this because obviously no money ever changes hands, but somehow money comes in and you have to manage the money and there's all sorts of stuff going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Paul, just give me a sec here, okay? I'm going to take over. Craig, how do you make money?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So I started a membership—

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: The program. Okay. I've been writing a newsletter, newsletters for 15, 16 years. And eight years ago, seven and a half years ago, I hit a wall.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And I couldn't get anything published where I wanted to get it published. And I thought I'm worthless and I have no value and I should go live in a dumpster.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: But membership programs were becoming a thing. And I wrote a piece for Wired about how digital books didn't become what we thought they were going to be, but membership stuff and online publishing is kind of more interesting than—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The Substack world.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, right at the beginning of that Substack stuff. And so I launched a membership program.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Your own?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: My own thing, because I'm pathologically incapable of being on anyone else's platform. And launched that and then from that I defined a weird permission to start going on these huge walks that I wanted to do in Japan. So I walked from Tokyo to Kyoto three times, I've walked thousands of kilometers all over other parts of Japan.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And I walk and I do these pop-up newsletters and the membership program essentially funds it. It gives me permission to go do these because people are paying me.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You've got enough of an audience paying you, okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And then from those, I write books, basically. As I'm walking, I'll do a walk for eight hours a day, I'll talk to people along the road, photograph, I take notes and then every night I write two, three thousand words. And then that goes out every night and so it's this weird aesthetic practice that I'll do for 40 days in a row. And then you end up with this corpus of 60, 70 thousand words at the end of it and all these photos and then you can kind of distill that into a book. And that's kind of been the process over the last six, seven years.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Very neat.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And I had a book come out with Random House last year, which was like kind of making me official in a way, you know? People suddenly go, as long as you can say like Random House, then people go, "Oh you are a writer now. This is real."

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So okay, so you got sources of income from the membership—members paying you to be members of your org?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So membership, book sales, that's it basically. Yeah, that's the big income.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Right, and so a little messy.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: A little messy, well because I live in Japan, I have, you know, whatever, you have bank accounts in both countries, America and Japan.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Ah I see, so you've got that.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I'm a US citizen and you always have to file in America even though there's a tax treaty, so all the taxes I pay, I pay primarily 100% in Japan, then those get carried over as part of the tax treaty so you can offset your American taxes with that, but you still have to go through the filing process. It's just, it stinks. America's the only country in the world except for one other little country that makes you do this if you're not a resident but you have citizenship, you have to file the whole thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But you love this. This is fun. You love taxes. I know about this one.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: No...

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: There is the amount of anxiety I have in January every year...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Panic attacks...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Really?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I'm, they're going to take everything away from me. I'm going to lose everything. You don't understand the scarcity mindset that like just does not disappear.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We understand. When I got married, my wife found a box of receipts and papers. She said and it was like—it was like a year to unpack everything that had not been dealt with, you know.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: No, the scarcity mindset just... it's just crippling and—

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: To be clear, I'd paid everything and added up...

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: ...like I had like $3,000 you know in fees...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, I know exactly. We all have this common backstory.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I, you know, every year I'm kind of going 'Oh my god, it's this thing again.' And like I, look, I hire accountants, you know, I try to keep digital records of everything, and yet every year I'm like I don't know what's going to happen, I can't see anything.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Gonna lose everything. Gonna lose everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. They're going to come down on me with a hammer because I missed one form or something, you know.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But nothing beats anxiety generation more than like tax prep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It really is terrible. No, because and then you... I don't know if you've had this experience. You go to the accountant and it's like going to a mixture of priest and therapist. They're like 'No, this is not...' You go in being like 'I may be the worst person who's ever lived on earth.' And they're like 'Eh, eh, eh.' And then they're done. And you're like 'That's it? Like there's no part where you tell me that I should be ashamed?' And they're like 'No, you owe the government like $480.' You're like 'Oh, okay, so I've been there, I know what this is.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But don't the accountants give you that relief? Like Marshes gives you that relief?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Well they do, but the organizing of all that... so it's like you know I'm bringing mine to Japan and like you know I've got I'm getting some payments over here, some payments over there. You know, and it's like I've got the pile of stuff that comes out of the year of work or whatever is non-trivial. And I'm producing books in Japan so I'm printing and shipping from Japan so I'm an exporter. So it means I have to file other forms as an exporter and I can get sales tax back—anyway.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So we got three things going. I have tremendous terrible anxiety about taxation, which you know about. Money in general. Just money in general.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We have 'I have a genuinely complicated international tax situation.' And we have 'When faced with frustrating situations, I build large web platforms.' And it sounds like Claude Code showed up and now all three of those things could get worked out at once.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, basically. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So in January I was like 'Well, what...' And I have very strong opinions about software. I kind of feel like I'm like to a certain degree I'm like a software super taster. I have I just I'm so sensitive to the way software works and I can kind of feel if the guts of it have been, you know, if the back sides have been painted.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: What's good lately? Before we get to your stuff, what's good lately?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I mean Things is still good. Things, okay. I just every time I use Things it like never lets me down, it just feels like a really wonderful...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's been around for forever. Been around for almost 20 years I think.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We talked to you once 10 years ago and you said uh it wasn't software but you're like 'the Brother laser printer, now that is a piece of software' or a piece of hardware. It just works. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: That also, yeah. It just works. Yeah, yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I still got I everywhere I move if I'm if I'm living somewhere for more than three weeks I just buy a Brother and put it in there.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Interesting. So so right now we you so about 0.000001% of software and hardware is good so far.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's it doesn't go too much further than that does it.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So I've been using Quicken, and Quicken's terrible. And then so my...

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Terrible.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Why is Quicken terrible?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Well, it doesn't... for someone like me, it's... it quick lacks imagination. All this software lacks imagination. They can't imagine a person like me existing in the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, always.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And it... and so it has no affordances for someone like me. And so I have to jump through all these wild hoops, you know, and I've got Google Sheets, and then I run Google scripts to, you know, normalize the data from my Japanese bank accounts that are in Google Sheets, and then I export that, and then I can bring that into Quicken and make a fake, you know, bank account in Quicken for that. But then everything's in dollars, and so the currency conversion's going back... anyway.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So this is like you have to read forum posts to learn how to use your accounting software.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's just not designed for it. It's just... it is. It's a narrow... a very narrow thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So this is the thing about Claude is you can just imagine the thing you want in the weirdest possible shape you want it, and it can make it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And so I just thought, okay, well, how... how long would it really take to rebuild Quicken to do this for me? And so I just started working on it, and it turns out it was like six days, basically.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Six days.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So... so what did you have to tell Claude?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: So I dumped... what did I... how did we start? I have all of my taxes in this folder for the last like 20 years or whatever, and I kind of just launched Claude in the... in the root directory.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hey buddy, you like my confidential tax information? Come to.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I don't care. Last year... last year I dumped anything my accountant sent me. I just put it straight into... then it was ChatGPT I was using. And it found serious... it found errors to the point where I fired my accountant last year.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: And it was like, here's a... here's a non-trivial amount of money that you should not be losing on this, you know, you should not be paying.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Was it accurate or did it hallucinate someone else's taxes?

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: No, no, it was... it was... it was accurate. It was...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: I mean, this is the thing is like you just... you're not... it's not telling you something that you just give to the IRS. You're filtering it through people. And like my accountant was like, oh yeah, you're right. And I was like, great, you're fired.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean that's so okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yikes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: This is terrible.

full_transcript.speaker: Craig Mod
full_transcript.text: No, but the... last year, the real insight was all of this anxiety I have about money, I can use ChatGPT as a therapist for it. So I would say to ChatGPT, like, I'm really worried that I'm making a mistake here and I'm going to lose all my money. What's like the worst possible scenario that can... it'd be like, what are you talking about? highlights: Craig Mod used AI to build a custom, bespoke accounting system in just six days to handle complex international tax and banking issues that mainstream software like Quicken failed to address.

The process of building software is shifting from line-by-line coding toward high-level architectural understanding, allowing creators to "paint" applications by describing logic rather than manually writing every function.

Fields like law, accounting, and programming are particularly susceptible to AI disruption because their outputs are essentially verifiable "code," which may force a fundamental shift in white-collar identity and professional value.

Current AI tools represent a massive leap in individual capability—described as moving from "candlelight to stadium lighting"—enabling a single person to stand up complex platforms that previously required large teams.

Despite the potential for significant job displacement, there is an optimistic view that automating routine intellectual labor will force humanity to contend with deeper questions of meaning and creative purpose.
summary:
In this episode, Paul Ford and Rich Ziade interview designer, writer, and programmer Craig Mod about his decision to build a custom accounting platform using AI tools like Claude Code. Living in Japan as a US citizen with complex income streams from book sales and memberships, Mod found commercial software like Quicken "lacked imagination" and failed to address his unique needs. In just six days, he developed a bespoke dashboard that automates data ingestion and serves as a tool to mitigate the "crippling anxiety" associated with international tax compliance. The project illustrates a significant shift in software development, where individuals can now bypass generic corporate tools to create highly specific, "super-taster" level software tailored to their exact workflows.

The conversation eventually expands into a broader philosophical debate regarding the future of work and human identity. While AI significantly lowers the barrier to coding, the group emphasizes that successful implementation still requires "architectural knowledge" and a deep understanding of how software components snap together—skills often honed over decades. Using the metaphor of 1938 and the dawn of the nuclear age, Mod describes AI as a powerful force that humanity is still struggling to understand. Despite concerns about AI "eating" white-collar professions like law and accounting, the speakers remain cautiously optimistic, suggesting that human messiness, creativity, and resilience will allow society to navigate this technological "natural disaster" toward a more meaningful future.
